nodes.context: Vietnamese criminal organization that started as river pirates in the 1920s, later became a military force and dominated opium distribution in Southern Vietnam.
Criminal groups from Macau that ran casinos in Saigon before being forcibly taken over by Bảy Viễn.
Chinese triad operating in Chợ Lớn, Saigon, during the 1960s. Tín Mã Nàm was the second highest-ranking member.
Criminal group led by Đại Cathay, one of the Four Great Kings of Saigon in the 1960s, involved in prostitution, nightclubs, drug dens, and casinos.
Criminal group led by Tín Mã Nàm, a Chinese crime boss and triad member in Chợ Lớn during the 1960s.
Criminal organization led by Năm Cam, dominant in Saigon during the 1990s, involved in illegal gambling and bribery.

edges.evidence_quote: Bảy Viễn forcibly took control of the casinos from Macanese organized crime groups.
edges.context: Bình Xuyên forcibly took over casinos from Macanese groups.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1964, a bloody gang battle ensued between the two gangs when Đại and his followers attacked the Great World quarters of Chợ Lớn with knives, swords, and bayonets.
edges.context: Đại Cathay's gang fought a gang war with Tín Mã Nàm's gang in 1964.

edges.evidence_quote: Tín Mã Nàm was then forced to call for a negotiation with Đại, where he and many of the Chinese gangs in Chợ Lớn decided to give up away the areas between Nancy market and District 1 to Đại Cathay's gang.
edges.context: After the conflict, Tín Mã Nàm negotiated and ceded territory to Đại Cathay.

edges.evidence_quote: Đại Cathay would go on to leave a lasting mark on Năm Cam, a mobster who would go on to later build his own fiefdom in Saigon during the 90s, and he did it mainly through his casino business, a method which he learned during his time of following Đại Cathay.
edges.context: Năm Cam learned casino business methods from Đại Cathay, indicating a mentorship or influence relationship.
